history: Use publicly available real dataset (e.g., Kaggle mobile app events, Google Analytics sample data) → Tableau or PowerBI for rapid prototyping with built-in funnel and flow templates
loo_notes: Real dataset + built-in templates tick the boxes, but Tableau/PowerBI won't teach custom interactive design the way D3.js would—you're outsourcing the skill-building. The 10-day window is tight enough that rapid templating makes sense, but the student will miss the chance to build publication-ready, fully custom visualizations that transfer to real-world dataviz work.

history: Use publicly available real dataset (e.g., Kaggle mobile app events, Google Analytics sample data) → D3.js or Observable for custom, publication-ready interactive visualizations
loo_notes: Strong tool choice (D3.js/Observable) and real data source hit the gold standard, and Sankey is the right pick for sequential user flows. The gap: the plan doesn't specify which two visualization types or commit to interactivity—filtering or linked views across the suite would push this from solid to standout for 10 days. Add one sentence on the second viz type and interactivity.

history: Generate realistic synthetic event-log data from scratch with defined user personas and journey patterns → Sankey diagram (paths/flows) + heatmap (temporal drop-off by stage)
loo_notes: Sankey + heatmap is a solid pair that checks all boxes—sequential flow + temporal insight—but synthetic data without real-world validation costs you scope credibility. On a 10-day deadline, you're spending 2–3 days generating and documenting the synthetic personas when a public dataset (Kaggle mobile events, GA export) would let you spend that time on interactivity—filtering by user segment, drill-down, or linked selection between views. The plan is safe and achievable, but it doesn't push toward the gold standard of real data + custom interactive D3 that would make this portfolio-worthy.

history: Generate realistic synthetic event-log data from scratch with defined user personas and journey patterns → Funnel chart (conversion by stage) + sequence diagram (power-user vs casual paths)
loo_notes: Synthetic data generation will eat 3-4 days of your 10-day budget just validating that it's realistic enough—that's a red flag for scope fit. More critically: a funnel chart is static and doesn't show *where* users drop off or *which paths* lead to conversion; a sequence diagram is vague and risky to execute in 10 days without a proven tool. You need D3.js or Observable + a Sankey (or similar flow viz) to actually *see* the journey branches. Pick a real Kaggle dataset instead and spend those days on interactivity.
